ground_truth.title: Ben Cullen Williams: Rethinking Portraiture
ground_truth.author: Emma Jacob
ground_truth.main_content: Ben Cullen Williams (b. 1988) is a London-based artist working across sculpture, installation, photography and video. His practice explores the relationship between space, technology and landscape, drawing on both physical and digital processes to interrogate how material systems are shaped, mediated and transformed. In 2021, Williams was longlisted for the Aesthetica Art Prize for Living Archive: An AI Performance Experiment, a collaboration with Google Arts & Culture and choreographer Wayne McGregor. His latest project, Self Portrait, was developed in collaboration with Google and DeepMind Research Scientist Jason Baldridge. For the work, Williams prompted a generative AI to describe itself before training the model on his own images, producing a self-portrait that merges the machine's self-image with the artist's vision. The project revisits the centuries-old question of what a self-portrait can be, reframing it for an era of generative systems. Williams studied architecture before doing an MA in Sculpture at the Royal College of Art. His interests stem from his initial studies in architecture and his larger sculptural works borrow visual language from that discipline. He has always taken photographs: from a young age he had point and shoot cameras, which he still uses now. Constellations of photographs that he has taken are the bedrock of his practice. Much of his work explores the human-made alterations of our natural landscapes and corporeal world, either looking at technological alterations in his subject or using technology as the medium to change these landscapes further. He collaborated with Google Arts & Culture in the past and met research scientist Jason Baldridge who was developing AI image models at Google. They started discussing possible projects and how they might be interesting to artists. Williams was curious to know what the image model might know about itself and whether it would be able to reveal the complexities of its own system. Through working with DeepMind, they were able to fine-tune a series of image models on his photographs, creating pieces that felt incredibly human, with flaws and imperfections. When using any technological medium that has its own rules, Williams navigates those to suit what he is trying to achieve. He aims to question the subject with the medium itself so, when using AI, he is actually questioning the medium of AI. The work sits in a grey area between the two: the subject is AI, the knowledge of itself comes from AI, the medium is AI, however, it has been trained on his photographs, he curated the prompts, he edited the film and worked with the composers and crucially the concept is his. Williams is currently redeveloping a work created with Sir Wayne McGregor, which will be presented in an exhibition at Somerset House. He is also continuing an image and film based project started while on a residency at the base of Mount Etna while it was erupting.
with: Ben Cullen Williams (b. 1988) is a London-based artist working across sculpture, installation, photography and video.
His latest project, Self Portrait, was developed in collaboration with Google and DeepMind Research Scientist Jason Baldridge.

For the work, Williams prompted a generative AI to describe itself before training the model on his own images, producing a self-portrait that merges the machine's self-image with the artist's vision.
The work certainly sits in a grey area between the two: the subject is AI, the knowledge of itself comes from AI, the medium is AI.
We need to continue to work with AI through projects that contain criticality, to form new understandings and thoughtful engagement with the rapidly developing technologies.
